claim.label: Plurality Decay
claim.query: protestant presbyterian minority church of ireland mixed civic labour
claim.counter_query: volunteer patriotic gaelic discipline sacrifice nation
claim.thesis: Measure the decline of confessional and civic plurality inside the press slice of the archive.

are very largely manufactured in Belfast, where there are

great clay beds for tiles, bricks, terra cotta, etc.

The foregoing remarks deal with articles that are

specially mentioned to be the manufacture of foreign

firms, but I would draw attention to the following large

sums of money which are specified to be spent, and also

the note describing how they are to be dealt with.

the' company’s staff or the company’s premises for the

purpose of gathering in proxies is very proper. ~We

do not see why Mr. Frederick C. Pilkington should not

occupy some of his spare time in addressing the enve-

lopes containing the requests to shareholders for their

proxies; and if, in consideration of a small fee, he got

permission from the National Assurance Co., of which

he is a distinguished director, to have the Great S. and

W. proxies in favour of him and his colleagues ad-

dressed fo him care of the Assurance Co., it would be a

very satisfactory arrangement, and the fee paid for the

accommodation would go towards reducing, in some

small way, the heavy call which unhappy widows,

orphans, and other distressed shareholders of that com-

supporting_documents.chunk_text:
‘see the coremony, and I heard that they were almost

beside the bridal party, paying the greatest attention,

of course, to the London and Paris fashions, as well as,

and there’s the rub, to the observance of the Protestant

Ritual, utterly regardless of the law of the Church for-

bidding them to be there.

Cahir, I said, is a “Bung’’ stronghold. ~ For the

2,058 inhabitants of this town of “ Gallant ’’ Tipperary,

there arve no less than about thirty-three or thirty-five

“bonded stores’’ that cost the town’s people and their

neighbours over £12,000 yearly, a fact that made me

often wonder does every man, woman and child in

Cahir drink “bung.”” I should not be surprised if

I were told they do. Sunday is a thirsty day in many

places in Ireland. It is especially so in Cahir. I

supporting_documents.chunk_text:
Ireland. According to “Thom’s,” it would seem “The

Church” 1is the Episcopalian Protestant Chapel. We

find under Booterstown that “Its buildings are, a

handsome Church, with square towers and pinnacles,

and lofty spire, a large Roman Catholic Church . . .7

Under Bray we find: “The old Parish Church, now St.

Paul’'s Chapel of Ease, stands on the high ground, near

the bridge. The new Parish Church is in the upper

outskirts of the town. There are also two Roman

Catholic Churches " Under Clondalkin we

read, “The only public buildings are the Parish Church,

a small plain modern building, and a neat Roman

Catholic Church.” Under Crumlin, “The Church 1s a

plain building; the Roman Catholic Church a neat

edifice.’ Under Finglas, “The only public build-

contradictory_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XI NO. 13, NOVEMBER 18 1905
contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
[page 8]
2 THE LEADER.

Temain after the Trusts declared had been fulfilled.

And from the letter of his Trustees to him, dated the

7th of August, 1681, and his reply to them in the same

year, we know that he sought to transfer, by Deed,

another slice of the Endowment to Christ’s Hospital.

Evidently with a view to this purpose, he urged the

Trustees to raise the rents, and to give no lease, except

in Limerick, and for building, longer than three years,

until the rents should reach £600. For this purpose

. also—namely, to increase the surplus to be carried away

by him, he reduced the number of Free Schools from 5

to 3. And although he mado this reduction on the

pleas of clothing poor children in the schools, and of

apprenticing them, we know from the Trustees’ letter

contradictory_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. X NO. 25, AUGUST 12 1905
contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
is also the Boerenbond, or Peasant’s League, something

like our Agricultural Societies here, founded by the

Abbé Mellaerts some years ago. Its object is to pro-

mote the moral and material interests of agriculturists,

to improve land legislation, to promote agricultural co-

operation. There is the Aumoniers du Travail, a

society of priests instituted by the bishop of Liege, who

make a special study of social questions, and promote in

their parishes, as circumstances demand or opportunity

offers, economic and social institutions for the better-

ment of the people. They have established working-

men’s hostels in the great industrial centres. Then

there are two great social organisations, both with a

common object, but seeking to reach it by different

contextual_chunks.text:
o ‘DﬁCEMEtp 30, 1905.

Jjot more than their rights, there would bo less of this
talk—this “electioneering dodge,”” as Lord Dunraven
might call it. The “Idolators’ have been so tame and
hmorogs that the exploiters of this “ electioneering
dodge instinctively feel that it is safe to play. this
trump card about “ Romish ** persecution if Home Rule
existed. So long as these bigots and oxploiters of this

electioncering dodge ’’ try to. keep, Ireland from her
rights by shouts of this kind, it is all the more incum-
bent on the persecuted majority of this country to man:
fully defend their just civic rights, and fight for them.
The utterly sectarian Mr. Sloan can stand for “ Pro-
testantism *’ over and above all; but if a base “Idola-
tor’” asks a question concerning the secular rights of un-
fur@unnte employés of the Great “Sick’’ and Sourface
Railway, Brother Goulding, of the Freemasons, and
others cry out against “dragging religion into business.’””
The more weak people foolishly “prove their toler-
ance,”” the more they may expect to bo jumped on ; when
they stand up manfully to their just fight they will very
quickly be treated with.

The Mungret Annual for

maz season of the year s usually one of gladness and re-
Joleing, it is also only too often a period of moaning and
groaning for those afilicted with coughs, colds, pains,
aches, and the many other ills to which the flesh is heir.
As one lays in a stock of good things to comfort the
}m@y and to appease and to please the appetite, so, too,
T is wiso to lay in a preventative against ailments.”’
Alas poor Pink! Bub away with all these thoughts
about pains and aches; the next shop visited was—well,
the reader can judge for himself what it was:—The
well-known licensed establishment of , which has
long since made its name in Dublin, continues to enjoy
a bigh reputation for the excellence of its liquors, and
e purity and good condition of its stouts and beers.”’
